headline: One Man's Decade-Long Mission to Preserve Veterans' Voices
meta_description: A 28-year-old has spent 10 years recording 3,000 World War II veterans' stories, creating a national archive before the Greatest Generation is gone.
summary_short: In a decade-long project, Rishi Sharma has preserved 3,000 World War II veterans' stories—a race against time as their numbers dwindle from 700,000 to 30,000.
body:
What began as youthful curiosity has become a race against history. Over the past ten years, 28-year-old Rishi Sharma has devoted himself almost entirely to one mission: recording the voices of World War II veterans before they're lost forever.

Starting in southern California, Sharma set out to interview as many combat veterans as possible. The scale of his commitment is striking—he has spent every single day of the last decade conducting these interviews, filling hard drives and SD cards with thousands of irreplaceable accounts. His collection now stands at 3,000 recorded stories.

The urgency of his work is impossible to ignore. When CBS News' Steve Hartman first profiled Sharma in 2016, there were roughly 700,000 WWII veterans still living in America. Today, that number has plummeted to just 30,000. Recently, Sharma completed an interview with a 100-year-old survivor of Iwo Jima—a conversation that might never have been captured without his persistence.

Sharma describes the veterans he meets as the "moral compass" of society, and his archive has become something of a national treasure. In preserving their stories, he's ensuring that the wisdom, sacrifice, and humanity of the Greatest Generation won't vanish with time. It's a quiet act of historical importance, one conversation at a time.
